result:
The old maple tree outside my library window is a repository of forgotten minutes. I come here not just for the books, but for the quality of its silence—a soft, dusty quiet, punctuated by the rustle of a turned page or the distant hum of the city. It is a place where time thickens and slows.

Today, my gaze keeps drifting to the tree. A squirrel is navigating its architecture with frantic, sure-footed purpose, a blur of brown against the grey March sky. Its world is so immediate: the next branch, the hidden acorn, the chattering rival. Watching it, I am struck by the sheer, unselfconscious *being* of it. There is no past clinging to its paws, no future clouding its focus. It is entirely in the negotiation between claw and bark.

We, of course, are burdened and blessed with the long view. We carry yesterday’s conversations and tomorrow’s anxieties like stones in our pockets. But sometimes, in a stolen moment like this, there is a gift in the forgetting. To watch the world without the need to document, analyze, or own it. To simply witness the ballet of the squirrel, the slow reach of the tree’s bare branches, the way the afternoon light, weak but persistent, gilds the spines of a thousand unread stories on the shelves.

The squirrel darts out of sight. The light shifts. I take a slow breath of the quiet air, feeling the weight in my pockets lighten, just a little, before I turn back to my own page.

Its world is so immediate: the next branch, the hidden acorn, the chattering rival. Watching it, I am struck by the sheer, unselfconscious *being* of it. There is no past clinging to its paws, no future clouding its focus. It is entirely in the negotiation between claw and bark.

We, of course, are burdened and blessed with the long view. We carry yesterday’s conversations and tomorrow’s anxieties like stones in our pockets. But sometimes, in a stolen moment like this, there is a gift in the forgetting. To watch the world without the need to document, analyze, or own it. To simply witness the ballet of the squirrel, the slow reach of the tree’s bare branches, the way the afternoon light, weak but persistent, gilds the spines of a thousand unread stories on the shelves.
